answer:
**In Chapter 1 of the *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, the dharma of the householder (*gṛhastha*) is described in section 5, titled "The Duties of a Householder (*gṛhasthadharmaprakaraṇam*)," spanning verses Yj_1.97–128.[^1]**

This section details the householder's daily routine, including ritual obligations (such as the *mahāyajñas*, or five great sacrifices, in vv. 102–104), hospitality to guests (vv. 107–111), feeding dependents (v. 105), Vedic study and worship (vv. 99–101), varṇa-specific duties (vv. 118–120), and general conduct like non-violence and sense-control (v. 122). It begins:

> [p-6c5d0a] A householder should daily perform the Smārta rites in his nuptial fire, or in the fire brought at the time of the division of the inheritance; and the Śrauta rites in the Vaitānika fires. // Yj_1.97 //[^2]

The passage emphasizes the householder's role as the foundation supporting other āśramas, through purity, charity, and ritual observance, before transitioning to the duties of the *snātaka* (graduate) in section 6.[^1]

[^1]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, ch. 1, section 5 (*gṛhasthadharmaprakaraṇam*), vv. 97–128, [full chapter](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Yājñavalkya Smṛti*, ch. 1, [¶6c5d0a](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-6c5d0a).
